claims.claim_text: 30 browser tabs alone can eat 4-6 GB, and once RAM is full the OS starts swapping to disk and everything becomes painfully slow.
claims.span_hint: section: RAM: Your Counter Space; pause-and-predict callout
claims.rationale: This is a numeric performance claim about browser memory usage and swap-related slowdown, so it needs a measurement-backed source.

claims.claim_text: In 2017, a GitLab engineer accidentally deleted a production database, and recovery depended on replicas and backups.
claims.span_hint: section: RAM: Your Counter Space; paragraph after swapping explanation
claims.rationale: This is a concrete outage incident and recovery claim, so it requires an incident-style source.

claims.claim_text: Windows was the most common desktop OS worldwide at 60.8% in March 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: The Operating System: Your Restaurant Manager; OS list block
claims.rationale: This is a dated market-share statistic and must be cited with a source that tracks OS usage share.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes worker nodes can run either Linux or Microsoft Windows, while the control plane stays on Linux.
claims.span_hint: section: The Operating System: Your Restaurant Manager; paragraph after OS list block
claims.rationale: This is a platform support statement tied to upstream Kubernetes documentation and should cite the official docs.

claims.claim_text: Cloud pricing depends on instance type, operating system, region, and purchase model; AWS listed a Linux t2.large with 8 GiB RAM in us-east-1 at $0.0928/hour on April 15, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Matters for Kubernetes; final paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a dated vendor pricing claim with specific SKU, region, OS, and hourly rate details.

claims.claim_text: A modern smartphone has more computing power than the computers NASA used for the 1969 moon landing, and the Apollo Guidance Computer had 74 KB of memory.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; first bullet
claims.rationale: This is a historical hardware-capacity comparison with a specific memory figure that needs sourcing.

claims.claim_text: Early computers used magnetic-core memory; each ring stored one bit, and a full megabyte would require over 8 million rings.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; second bullet
claims.rationale: This is a historical technology description with a concrete capacity calculation that should be cited.

claims.claim_text: SSDs have no moving parts and are therefore faster, quieter, and more durable than HDDs; dropping a laptop with an HDD may lose data while an SSD is less likely to.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; third bullet
claims.rationale: These are concrete storage-technology capability and durability claims that need a technical reference.

claims.claim_text: In 1947, engineers working on the Harvard Mark II found a moth in a relay, logged it as the first actual case of a bug being found, and the term debugging has been used ever since.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; fourth bullet
claims.rationale: This is a named historical anecdote about the origin story of debugging and should be sourced.

claims.claim_text: A team might pay about $400/month for a 32 GB RAM cloud server when the application only uses 2 GB, and right-sizing can save thousands of dollars.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes; row: Over-provisioning cloud servers
claims.rationale: This is a concrete cloud-cost example with a dollar figure and savings implication, so it needs pricing support or should be softened.

further_reading.title: Computer
further_reading.why_relevant: Good broad refresher on the major parts of a computer and how they fit together.

further_reading.title: Random-access memory
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful follow-up for understanding volatile memory, capacity, and why swapping hurts performance.

further_reading.title: Windows in Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Connects the module's OS discussion to the Linux/Windows reality students will see in Kubernetes.

notes: Excluded analogy-only prose, quiz/exercise content, and repeated claims inside quiz answers. StatCounter and the original GitLab postmortem host are outside the allowlist, so the proposed URLs for those claims are allowlisted anchor pages for follow-up sourcing.
